turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some... issues with my stomach for a while now. To be honest, I'm getting pretty frustrated because it feels like it's only getting worse.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's this crampy pain in my lower abdomen, mostly on the left side. It's been going on for a few weeks now, and it's getting to the point where I'm having trouble sleeping at night because of it. And on top of that, I've also noticed I'm going to the bathroom way more often than usual - like 8 or 10 times a day instead of my normal 1 or 2. The stool is pretty loose and watery, and I feel really urgent about getting there too. It's affecting my work performance because I'm always worried that I'll need to run out of the office in the middle of something important.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah. I've been feeling pretty fatigued lately, even when I'm not having an episode. It's like my energy levels are just really low all the time, and it's affecting my work because I'm normally a high-functioning guy who can handle long hours, but now I'm just struggling to keep up.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: To be honest, no. The only thing that really gets my attention is the urgency to use the bathroom. That's what gets me out of bed at night and makes it hard for me to focus during the day. I don't feel hot or cold, I don't sweat... it's just this constant feeling like I need to be near a toilet.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, that's correct. No sweating or clamminess for me, just a lot of discomfort and urgency with the bowel movements.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: It kind of stays in my lower left abdomen, but sometimes it feels like it radiates up into my upper belly. It's hard to explain, but it can feel like a band is tightening across my whole stomach area when I have an episode.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: No, it doesn't really radiate that far. It's mostly just in my lower abdomen and sometimes up into my upper belly, but it never goes beyond that for me.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, there's no tingling or numbness in my arms. Like I said, it's mostly just a crampy pain and this feeling of urgency to use the bathroom.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, there's no tingling or numbness in my arms. Like I said, it's mostly just a crampy pain and this feeling of urgency to use the bathroom. The only thing that's been affecting me is my energy levels - I'm just really tired all the time, even when I'm not having an episode.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: Actually, yeah. I've also been noticing some achy pain in both my knees and ankles lately. It's more of a dull ache than anything else, but it's definitely been bothering me. There's no swelling or redness that I can see, though.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: Honestly, no, my shoes don't feel tighter than usual and I haven't noticed any puffiness in my legs. But to be honest, I have been feeling pretty fatigued lately, even when I'm not having an episode. It's like my energy levels are just really low all the time, and it's affecting my work because I'm normally a high-functioning guy who can handle long hours, but now I'm just struggling to keep up.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah. Sometimes when I have an episode, I'll feel kind of queasy or nauseous afterwards. It's not always there, but it can definitely make me feel pretty lightheaded for a bit after I've gone to the bathroom. And like I said earlier, the urgency to use the bathroom is what really gets my attention - that and the crampy pain in my lower abdomen.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yeah, sometimes when I have an episode, I'll feel kind of queasy or nauseous afterwards. It's not always there, but it can definitely make me feel pretty lightheaded for a bit after I've gone to the bathroom.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah. I have been having low-grade fever episodes a few times a week - usually around 37.8-38.0°C. It's not super high, but it's definitely there and it's been happening pretty consistently for a while now. And actually, it's also been waking me up at night because of the nocturnal diarrhea I've been experiencing... like 2 or 3 times per week, I'll wake up in the middle of the night to use the bathroom and then have trouble falling back asleep afterwards.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah. I've actually lost about 4 kilograms over the past three weeks. It's a bit concerning because I'm not really feeling like I'm eating less than usual - if anything, my stomach issues have made me eat more frequently, but it's all just takeout and stuff because I don't have time to cook anymore with my work schedule.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet hasn't been great lately. With my long work hours, I've just been grabbing takeout or eating on the go most of the time. I'm not really tracking what I'm eating, but it's definitely not a balanced diet like it used to be when I had more free time. As for exercise, I try to get some walking in during my lunch break if I can, but it's not consistent and I've been feeling too fatigued to do much of anything else physically. My daily routine is pretty packed with work, so there's not a lot of downtime for me outside of sleeping.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, I try to get some walking in during my lunch break if I can, but it's not consistent. My schedule is pretty packed with work and I've been feeling too fatigued to do much of anything else physically lately.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet hasn't been great lately. With my long work hours, I've just been grabbing takeout or eating on the go most of the time. Sometimes it's a convenience store sandwich, sometimes it's a quick bite at a restaurant near the office. I'm not really tracking what I'm eating, but it's definitely not a balanced diet like it used to be when I had more free time.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's stuck out to me because she's been dealing with it for a while now and it's definitely on my radar when I think about what might be going on with me.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: Yeah, my sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's been a concern for me lately because of my symptoms, and it's definitely made me think about the possibility that I might be dealing with something similar.
